key_hits: Error 'Invalid credentials. Please try again.' verbatim
Blue 'Sign In' button with white text
Red error box with pale red background
key_misses: Describes traffic lights as a 'logo' rather than macOS window controls
gemma4-12b-Q8|think.justification: Comprehensive, well-organized description with all key text verbatim and accurate color/layout. Minor labeling imprecision on traffic lights (calls them logo, says orange instead of yellow) but no factual errors about image content.

hallucinations: Claims error message 'Invalid credentials. Please try again.' appears twice - it appears only once
Claims 'Forgot your password?' link appears twice - it appears only once
Claims two instances of the forgot-password link with one 'near the top (implied by the layout)' - no such link exists near top
gemma4-e4b-Q8|think.justification: Hallucination of duplicate elements is a serious failure. The model invents that both the error message and the forgot-password link appear twice when each appears exactly once. This is the worst failure mode per judging criteria. All actual OCR text is correct, but the invented duplicates are unacceptable.

The login form uses a standard sans-serif font. This mischaracterization of typography is a factual error about visible image content, warranting the hallucination flag.

notes: This login form is a relatively simple UI mockup with clear, legible text, so most models OCR'd all key strings correctly. The differentiator is hallucination: gemma4-e4b-Q8|think invented that the error message and forgot-password link each appear twice (worst failure), while qwen3.5-4b-Q8|think hallucinated a monospaced/typewriter font. The seven top-ranked models produced clean, accurate descriptions. Hex color codes offered by qwen3.5-4b-nothink and qwen3.5-9b-nothink are unverifiable but treated as minor overreach rather than hallucination since they don't misrepresent visible content. Six models correctly identified the macOS traffic-light window controls; gemma4-12b called them a 'logo' and glm-4.6v-flash-9b speculated they were a 'logo or status indicator' (both acceptable but less precise).
